front: Novel: Gregor's father throws an apple that lodges in his back, eventually causing his death; his sister Grete eventually suggests getting rid of him
Novel: the final sentence contains a German word meaning either 'traffic' or 'sexual intercourse'
Novel: three bearded lodgers leave after encountering the protagonist; Gregor imagines kissing his sister's neck as she plays violin
Novel: contains the parable 'Before the Law' in which a doorkeeper with a Tatar beard tells a man 'No one but you could gain admittance'; told by a priest to the protagonist
Novel: the protagonist is taken to a quarry by two officers and killed; exclaims 'Like a dog!'
Novel: the protagonist gets advice from sickly lawyer Herr Huld and cynical painter Titorelli; discovers policemen being flogged in a bank junkroom
Short Story: a torture device consisting of a Bed, Harrow, and Inscriber carves words into prisoners' bodies; inscribes 'Be Just' on the Officer's back before malfunctioning
Short Story: the old Commandant's grave is hidden under a table in a teahouse, with an inscription prophesying he will rise again; the new Commandant withholds funding
Novel: the land surveyor K. fails to gain access to the title structure; the elusive bureaucrat Klamm works there; messenger Barnabas relays letters
Short Story: a performer who starves himself professionally admits on his deathbed he never found food he liked; a panther replaces him in his cage
Short Story: narrated by the ape Red Peter, captured by the firm of Hagenbeck on the Gold Coast; learns to drink schnapps, spit, and smoke; chooses the Music Hall over freedom
back: A Report to an Academy (Franz Kafka)

front: Short Story: a mouse shortens her whistling-like performances and insists her singing is not 'piping'; during performances, Mouse Folk are able to dream
Short Story: Georg Bendemann's father lambasts his upcoming marriage to Frieda Brandenfeld and accuses him of wanting him dead; Georg jumps off a bridge
Short Story: an unnamed canine attempts to understand where food comes from; meets seven musical dogs walking on their hind legs; asks 'Whence does the earth procure this food?'
Short Story: explains the piecemeal construction of the title structure to protect from northern nomads; contains the embedded parable 'An Imperial Message' about a herald trapped in the palace
back: The Great Wall of China (Franz Kafka)

front: Short Story: about the mysterious Odradek, a 'flat star-shaped spool for thread' that Zizek calls 'jouissance embodied' because it serves no purpose
back: The Cares of a Family Man (Franz Kafka)

front: Short Story: a dead hunter who cannot die describes wandering on his boat to a burgomaster; W.G. Sebald references it in Vertigo
Short Story: the title character regrets answering 'A false alarm on the night bell' and imagines the rape of his maid Rosa by a man from a pigsty
Novel: Karl Rossmann arrives in the title country and sees the Statue of Liberty wielding a sword; the first chapter 'The Stoker' was published separately
Novel: in the last chapter, Karl Rossmann travels to the Nature-Theater of Oklahoma, which promises a job to anyone who applies
Author: wrote a vindictive 100-page Letter to His Father explaining why he broke off his engagement to Felice Bauer
Author: the most popular of his 109 Zurau Aphorisms is 'A cage went in search of a bird'
Author: David Foster Wallace's essay describes this author's humor as 'pounding at a door that opens outward: we've been inside what we wanted all along'
